ARTICLE_SUBTITLE: What Plato's allegory tells us about algorithmic reality
ARTICLE_EXCERPT: Plato imagined prisoners chained to shadows. We chose ours.
ARTICLE_IMAGE_ALT: A dark cave with light streaming through the entrance
ARTICLE_IMAGE_CAPTION: The entrance we rarely look for. Photo: Jane Doe.
ARTICLE_IMAGE_CAPTION_1: Shadows we accept as reality. Photo: Jane Doe.

ARTICLE_BODY.text: Plato's prisoners have never been more relatable. Chained since birth, they watch shadows dance on a stone wall and call it the world. We laugh — then open our feeds.
The algorithm is not malicious. It is merely a very efficient shadow-puppet master, optimised for the one metric that keeps us chained: attention.
Plato was careful to note that the prisoners do not know they are prisoners. The cave is all they have ever known, so it is, by definition, reality.
They see only their own shadows, or the shadows of one another, which the fire throws on the opposite wall of the cave. — Plato, The Republic
Substitute 'fire' with 'server farm' and the metaphor holds with uncomfortable precision.
Plato's freed prisoner stumbles into the sunlight, blinded and disoriented. He does not immediately celebrate his liberation — he wants to go back. The cave was comfortable.
Perhaps the question is not how to destroy the algorithm, but how to learn to look sideways — to notice the mechanism casting the shadows, even while watching them.
